title: Urban conservation gardening in the decade of restoration
methods: linear regression (occupancy vs. Ellenberg N values)
Mann-Whitney U test (cultivated vs. non-cultivated occupancy change)
spatial overlay analysis (protected areas + urban green spaces)
retail availability survey (n=100 Red List species)
data_sources: German vascular plant occupancy database (ref. 25, ~60-yr timeseries)
German Red List of vascular plants

data_scope.spatial_coverage: Germany (primary case study); global examples from New Zealand, oceanic islands, China
data_scope.time_period_ecology: approximately 1960-1987 baseline with ~60-year occupancy change window
data_scope.red_list_sample: 100 threatened species sampled (~10% of German Red List), 35% found available online
data_scope.urban_green_space_major_cities: ~65,000 ha across 50 major German cities; additional ~44,000 ha allotment gardens

core_conclusion: Cultivating declining native plant species in urban public and private green spaces (conservation gardening) is an economically viable, participatory approach that complements traditional habitat-protection methods. Using Germany as a case study, cultivated plants show higher occupancy trends over six decades, urban green spaces are spatially complementary to protected areas, and reforming the horticultural market through labelling and cross-sectoral native seed integration can scale this citizen-led conservation instrument to meet global biodiversity targets.
why_it_matters: Fills the gap between protected areas and biodiversity targets; turns urban dwellers into conservation actors at scale (55% and projected 92% global urban population); empirically grounded in 60-year occupancy data for 1,249 German plant species; provides a specific policy blueprint (conservation gardening label, horticultural market reform, EU funding pathways); directly responds to the UN Decade on Ecosystem Restoration and Post-2020 Global Biodiversity Framework.
why_nature: Integrates ecology, economics and policy across disciplines Nature Sustainability prizes; addresses a globally recognized biodiversity crisis with a scalable nature-based solution; Germany case study is framed as transferable to higher-income contexts worldwide with extensions to lower-income and island settings; participatory citizen-led approach aligns with Nature Sustainability's human-nature system coupling emphasis.
added_value_to_my_research: 2SFCA/R2SFCA applied to horticultural outlet accessibility -> mapping 'conservation gardening deserts' across urban socioeconomic gradients
Multi-source data fusion (iNaturalist/GBIF + remote sensing) -> citizen-science occupancy signal for fine-tuning geospatial foundation models
GWR/MGWR applied to green-amenity housing price uplift -> equity audit of conservation gardening label premium across income strata
Explainable ML (gradient boosting + SHAP) on species trait databases -> automated global triage of conservation gardening candidate species
Spatially-varying models linking urban plant beta-diversity to health outcomes -> GeoAI pipeline from biodiversity policy to population health impact

related.relation: citizen-science plant data for characterizing urban green heterogeneity — directly extends paper's occupancy monitoring approach

related.relation: social equity and green gentrification risk of nature-inclusive urban interventions — equity dimension of conservation gardening scale-up

related.title: Urban areas as hotspots for bees and pollination but not a panacea for all insects
related.relation: empirical evidence that urban areas can serve as biodiversity hotspots for pollinators — ecological co-benefit mechanism for conservation gardening

related.title: Beta-diversity of urban vegetation shapes microbial heterogeneity and maintains healthy environment
related.relation: urban plant diversity linked to human health via beta-diversity hypothesis — health co-benefit pathway for native plant conservation gardening

figures.caption: Positive association between grid-cell occupancy frequency (1960-1987) and Ellenberg N value for 1,249 German plant species — nutrient-demanding species occupy larger ranges
Boxplots showing cultivated native plants and neophytes have significantly higher occupancy change over six decades than non-cultivated species (Mann-Whitney U test)
Maps of Germany: (a) protected areas only; (b) protected + urban green spaces for 96 cities; (c) Berlin core — spatial complementarity and connectivity
Photo gallery of threatened German native species available from online retailers, with IUCN Red List category and example urban uses
Tiered species-selection framework: from specialist ex situ conservation to citizen-led mainstreaming, based on propagation viability and habitat requirements
